Example 1 — Left downwind Runway 16: landing direction is 160° magnetic; downwind is 160° + 180° = 340°, roughly northwest.

Example 2 — Midfield left downwind Runway 13: the aircraft is west of the runway centerline (pattern side opposite the runway numbers for a left pattern at this airport geometry). Knowledge-test items often list east, south, or west as choices—use the runway alignment and left-pattern rule to pick the correct quadrant.

Example 3 — Midfield left downwind Runway 4: same reasoning yields west of the runway for a typical left-hand pattern configuration as used in course materials.

Example 4 — Left downwind Runway 12: downwind heading = 120° + 180° = 300°.

Example 5 — Left downwind Runway 24: downwind heading = 240° − 180° = 60°.

Example 6 — Short final Runway 18: the aircraft is on final aligned with Runway 18 (magnetic course 180° toward the runway), which is generally north of the airport reference when Runway 18 is oriented to the south. Short final is the last segment of the final approach to touchdown.

sub_units.title: Traffic Patterns for Manned Aircraft
sub_units.description: Describe standard VFR traffic pattern flow, typical pattern altitudes near airports, and entry, downwind, final, and departure procedures.
sub_units.text_content:
Airports use a standard traffic pattern to organize takeoffs and landings. The pattern may differ when a control tower assigns non-standard flows, when terrain or obstacles require changes, or when pilots deviate for safety. Never assume every aircraft is flying the published left-hand pattern; if manned traffic is nearby, landing the sUAS until the area is clear may be the safest option.

Even though many propeller aircraft cruise at higher altitudes, they descend through lower altitudes near airports. On approach or departure paths, aircraft may be at roughly 500 to 1,500 feet AGL several miles from the field. Common pattern altitudes for light propeller aircraft are about 600 to 1,500 feet AGL.

Patterns are normally left-hand unless ATC assigns otherwise or the chart shows a right pattern with the (RP) symbol. Left-hand patterns are not specially labeled on the chart.

Significant pattern items:
- Enter the pattern in level flight, abeam the midpoint of the runway, at pattern altitude.
- 1,000 feet AGL is the recommended pattern altitude unless another altitude is published.
- Maintain pattern altitude until abeam the approach end of the landing runway on the downwind leg.
- Complete the turn to final at least one-quarter mile from the runway.
- After takeoff or go-around, continue straight ahead until past the departure end of the runway.
- If remaining in the pattern, begin the turn to crosswind beyond the departure end of the runway, within 300 feet of pattern altitude.
- If leaving the pattern, continue straight out or exit with a 45-degree turn beyond the departure end after reaching pattern altitude.

sub_units.title: Airport Location and Mandatory Instruction Signs
sub_units.description: Recognize location signs and mandatory instruction signs on airport movement areas and state when pilots must stop for ATC clearance.
sub_units.text_content:
Location signs have a black background with yellow letters and identify a taxiway or runway location.

Mandatory instruction signs have a red background with white letters. They mark locations where pilots must stop unless cleared by ATC—effectively a "do not enter" until cleared. They appear at runway intersections, critical areas, and other restricted zones.

Aircraft must not pass a mandatory instruction sign or associated holding position until ATC clears the movement.

sub_units.title: Direction, ILS Holding, and Runway Holding Markings
sub_units.description: Identify direction and destination signs, ILS critical area holding requirements, and runway holding position markings at taxiway and runway intersections.
sub_units.text_content:
Direction and destination signs have yellow backgrounds with black text and arrows and guide pilots to runways, terminals, cargo areas, and other airport locations.

ILS critical area holding position: When the ILS critical area is active—usually in low visibility—pilots must hold short as marked until the area is inactive or ATC clears the movement.

Runway holding position marking consists of two solid lines and two broken lines across the full width of the taxiway or runway. When approaching from the side with the continuous lines, do not cross without ATC clearance. These markings appear next to holding position signs at taxiway and runway intersections (for example, holding short of Runway 15 with Runway 33 to the right). Do not proceed beyond the holding position until cleared by ATC.

sub_units.title: Airport Security (SIDA)
sub_units.description: Explain Security Identification Display Area (SIDA) requirements and badge and movement rules under 49 CFR Part 1542.
sub_units.text_content:
If a drone operation requires you to enter airport property, determine whether that area is a Security Identification Display Area (SIDA).

Inside a SIDA you must wear a visible airport-issued identification badge and follow airport and TSA procedures under 49 CFR Part 1542. Movement in a SIDA without a displayed badge is prohibited.

sub_units.title: Wildlife Hazards Near Airports
sub_units.description: State reporting options for wildlife strikes, prohibitions on harassing wildlife, and immediate actions when wildlife threatens the operation.
sub_units.text_content:
Wildlife strikes are a serious hazard to manned aircraft. Remote pilots must not attract wildlife or increase strike risk near airports.

If your small unmanned aircraft collides with wildlife, you are not required to report it to the FAA, but you may report voluntarily to the FAA Wildlife Strike Database.

Harassing wildlife violates FAA policy and environmental laws. If you observe wildlife near your operation, land immediately and reassess before continuing.

units.title: WEATHER
units.sub_title: Aviation Weather Sources, Observations (METAR), and Forecasts (TAF)
units.description: By the end of this lesson on WEATHER, you will covers the essential aviation weather services, how to read surface observation reports like METAR, and how to use key forecast products such as TAF, SIGMET, and AIRMET for safe flight planning.
units.text_content: Weather is a critical part of preflight planning, requiring knowledge of specialized aviation weather sources, real-time observation reports, and various forecast products to ensure flight safety and informed decision-making.

sub_units.title: Aviation Weather Sources Overview
sub_units.text_content: Weather is an important part of preflight planning. The Weather Services system is a collaborative effort involving the National Weather Service (NWS), Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), Department of Defense (DOD), and other aviation organizations and individuals. Weather can vary greatly depending on location and altitude. Aviation weather reports are available at www.aviationweather.gov (http://www.aviationweather.gov) and 1800wxbrief.com (http://1800wxbrief.com). Weather forecasts are not 100% but are increasingly accurate, using scientific research and computer models to identify patterns, trends, and characteristics of weather systems. This information is used to support informed decision-making for flight planning and safety.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Aviation Weather Sources Overview, you will understanding the collaborative system of agencies providing aviation weather data and where to access official reports.

sub_units.title: Surface Aviation Weather Observations
sub_units.text_content: Surface Aviation Weather Observations include data from both government-operated and privately contracted facilities, providing real-time, localized weather data collected at ground stations across the US. Automated weather sources like Automated Weather Observing Systems (AWOS) and Automated Surface Observing Systems (ASOS) play major roles in data collection. AWOS is a collection of weather sensors that must meet FAA standards to be able to report weather factors. A Surface Observation Report contains: Type of report (routine or special), Station identifier (airport code or location), Date and time of the observation, Modifier, Wind speed and direction, Visibility (horizontal distance pilots can see), Runway Visual Range (RVR), Weather phenomena, Sky condition, Temperature and dew point, Altimeter reading (atmospheric pressure), and Remarks.
sub_units.description: Describe on real-time, localized weather data sources, including AWOS and ASOS, and the general content of a surface observation report.

sub_units.title: Aviation Routine Weather Report (METAR) Decoding
sub_units.text_content: Aviation Weather Reports provide accurate, current depictions of weather conditions to help pilots make informed decisions. The METAR (Aviation Routine Weather Report) is the standard international format for surface weather observations, typically issued hourly. The SPECI is a special METAR issued between routine reports when significant weather changes occur.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Aviation Routine Weather Report (METAR) Decoding, you will a breakdown of the METAR format, covering station identifiers, time conventions, wind reporting, visibility, sky condition, and temperature/altimeter settings.

sub_units.title: Report Type, Station, Date, and Time
sub_units.text_content: The report begins with the Type of report: METAR (regular) or SPECI (special report). This is followed by the Station identifier, a 4-letter code to identify airports and other aviation facilities. The Date & time of report is a 6-digit group (date + time) given in Zulu time (UTC), followed by 'Z'. The Modifier, if present, denotes an automated source ('AUTO') or a corrected report ('COR').
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Report Type, Station, Date, and Time, you will decoding the METAR header, including the report type (METAR/SPECI), station code, time format (Zulu), and modifiers (AUTO/COR).

sub_units.title: Wind and Visibility
sub_units.text_content: Wind is reported with direction (first three digits, blowing from in tens of degrees) and speed (last two or three digits) in knots. If the wind direction is variable, 'VRB' is used. Gusts are indicated by 'G' followed by the peak gust (e.g., G26KT). Visibility is reported in statute miles ('SM').
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Wind and Visibility, you will how wind direction, speed, gusts (G), variable winds (VRB), and visibility (SM) are reported in the METAR.

sub_units.title: Weather Phenomena and Sky Condition
sub_units.text_content: Weather is broken down into Qualifiers (intensity: light (-), moderate ( ), heavy (+), and proximity: 'VC' for 5–10 miles from the airport) and Descriptors (describing precipitation or obscurations). Sky condition is reported with amount (in eighths), height (3-digit number in hundreds of feet AGL), and type (e.g., TCU or CB). 'VV' (vertical visibility) is used if clouds cannot be seen due to fog or heavy precipitation.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Weather Phenomena and Sky Condition, you will interpreting weather qualifiers (intensity, proximity), descriptors, and sky condition reporting, including cloud height and type.

sub_units.title: Temperature, Altimeter, and Remarks
sub_units.text_content: Air Temperature and Dew Point are always given in °C. Temperatures below 0 °C are preceded by 'M' (Minus). The Altimeter setting is inches of mercury ('Hg') in a four-digit group, preceded by 'A'. Remarks start with 'RMK' and include various additional data like pressure information ('PRESRR' or 'PRESFR') and automated station maintenance indicators.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Temperature, Altimeter, and Remarks, you will the reporting format for temperature and dew point (°C, 'M' for minus), altimeter setting ('A'), and the content of the 'RMK' (Remarks) section.

sub_units.title: Aviation Weather Forecast Products
sub_units.text_content: Forecasts are essential for preflight planning. Pilots should be familiar with the various types of forecast products that provide anticipated weather conditions for specific areas and altitudes.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Aviation Weather Forecast Products, you will an introduction to the different types of weather forecasts essential for flight planning, including TAF, SIGMET, and AIRMET.

sub_units.title: Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) and Aviation Area Forecast (FA)
sub_units.text_content: The TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast for a specific airport (usually larger ones), covering expected weather within a 5-mile radius, valid for 24 or 30 hours and updated four times daily (0000Z, 0600Z, 1200Z, and 1800Z). The FA (Aviation Area Forecast) is a broad regional forecast.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) and Aviation Area Forecast (FA), you will specifics on the TAF (coverage, duration, update frequency) and the role of the FA (regional scope).

sub_units.title: SIGMET and Convective SIGMET (WST)
sub_units.text_content: SIGMET (Significant Meteorological Information) advisories are issued for severe weather hazards (e.g., turbulence, volcanic ash). Convective SIGMETs (WST) are issued specifically for severe thunderstorms producing surface winds greater than 50 knots, hail ¾ inch or larger, or the presence of tornadoes, or heavy precipitation affecting 40% or more of an area at least 3,000 square miles in size. They are relevant to all aircraft for safety and preflight planning.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on SIGMET and Convective SIGMET (WST), you will the criteria and hazards covered by SIGMETs and the specific conditions that trigger a Convective SIGMET (WST).

sub_units.title: AIRMET and Winds/Temperatures Aloft (FB)
sub_units.text_content: AIRMET (Airman’s Meteorological Information) advisories are for less severe weather conditions (e.g., moderate turbulence or icing). The FB (Winds and Temperatures Aloft Forecast) provides wind direction, speed, and temperature at various altitudes, which is crucial for determining flight path efficiency and conditions at altitude.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on AIRMET and Winds/Temperatures Aloft (FB), you will the difference between AIRMET (moderate conditions) and the use of the FB forecast (wind and temperature at various altitudes).

sub_units.title: Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) Decoding
sub_units.text_content: The TAF provides essential forecast information for specific airports and uses a coded structure that must be carefully decoded to understand the expected weather conditions.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF) Decoding, you will decoding the structure and content of a TAF report, including the valid period, wind, visibility, and forecast change groups like FM and TEMPO.

sub_units.title: TAF Header: Type, ID, and Origin Time
sub_units.text_content: The TAF includes the Type of report (Routine TAF or Amended TAF AMD), the ICAO station identifier (4-digit code, same as a METAR), and the Date and time of origin (6-number code in UTC/Zulu).
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on TAF Header: Type, ID, and Origin Time, you will identifying the routine or amended TAF, the station, and the time the forecast was issued.

sub_units.title: Valid Period and Core Forecast Elements
sub_units.text_content: The TAF Valid period dates and times (e.g., 0812/0912) indicate the forecast's duration. This is followed by the core forecast elements: Forecast wind (five-digit group), Forecast visibility (in statute miles, 'P6SM' for greater than six miles), Forecast significant weather, and Forecast sky condition (same format as METAR, but only Cumulonimbus (CB) clouds are forecast in this section).
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Valid Period and Core Forecast Elements, you will interpreting the start/end dates and times of the forecast and decoding the wind, visibility, weather, and sky condition elements.

sub_units.title: Forecast Change Groups (FM, TEMPO, PROB)
sub_units.text_content: The Forecast change group indicates expected significant weather changes. 'FM' (From) is used when a rapid and significant change is expected (usually within an hour). 'TEMPO' (Temporary) is for temporary fluctuations of weather expected to last less than 1 hour. 'PROB30' or 'PROB40' (Probability) is a percentage describing the probability of thunderstorms and precipitation, but this group is not used for the first 6 hours of a 24-hour forecast.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Forecast Change Groups (FM, TEMPO, PROB), you will understanding the difference between 'FM' (rapid change), 'TEMPO' (short fluctuation), and 'PROB' (probability of precipitation or thunderstorms).

units.title: WEATHER EFFECTS ON AIRCRAFT PERFORMANCE
units.sub_title: Understanding Weather's Impact on sUAS Operations
units.description: Explain how atmospheric pressure, density, temperature, humidity, wind, stability, clouds, storms, fronts, and mountain weather affect sUAS performance so you can plan flights and decide when conditions exceed safe or regulatory limits.
units.text_content: This unit connects weather to aircraft performance and risk. Pilots cannot control the weather—only their decisions. Every aircraft has weather limits set by the manufacturer; exceeding those limits increases the chance of loss of control, crashes, injury, or property damage. If weather exceeds what the aircraft can safely handle, do not fly. Routine jobs (for example, inspecting a roof) should be delayed when conditions are poor; only missions that truly justify it (for example, searching for a child whose life may be in danger) may warrant accepting higher weather risk. Most operations do not justify high weather risk. Pilots care about weather because it changes air density, which affects lift, thrust, stability, and battery endurance. The lessons that follow define weather, then build from pressure and density through wind, stability, moisture, icing, hazardous clouds and thunderstorms, fronts, mountain flying, and Part 107 ceiling and visibility minimums.

sub_units.title: What Is Weather?
sub_units.description: Define weather as the state of the atmosphere at a time and place and explain how uneven solar heating drives air movement and the basic elements (temperature, wind, pressure, moisture) that pilots track.
sub_units.text_content: Weather is the current condition of the atmosphere at a specific time and place. It includes temperature, wind, atmospheric pressure, moisture (humidity and precipitation), and the clouds and precipitation those ingredients produce. Weather happens because the sun heats the Earth unevenly; uneven heating causes air to move. Moving air transports heat and moisture and is the engine behind changing conditions at the surface and aloft.

sub_units.title: Air Pressure, Density, and Altitude Effects
sub_units.description: Explain how pressure and density change with altitude, define standard atmospheric references, and apply density altitude concepts to predict sUAS performance in high, hot, and humid conditions.
sub_units.text_content:
Air pressure is the weight of the air above you caused by gravity. More air above you means higher pressure; less air above you means lower pressure. As altitude increases, pressure decreases, and air density decreases. Atmospheric pressure is commonly reported in inches of mercury (inHg) or millibars (mb). Standard sea-level reference conditions are 29.92 inHg (1,013.2 mb) and 59°F (15°C), and FAA testing may use either pressure unit.

Air density is the number of air molecules in a given volume of air. High density (thick air) means more molecules, better lift, better propeller efficiency, and better performance. Low density (thin air) means fewer molecules, reduced lift and thrust, and worse performance.

Low air density is promoted by high altitude, high temperature, and high humidity (high-hot-humid). Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature and describes how the air feels to the aircraft. Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated when an altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg. Standard temperature is 15°C at sea level and decreases about 2°C (3.5°F) per 1,000 feet as altitude increases. When actual temperature is higher than standard, density altitude increases and performance decreases.

Key performance relationship: lower density altitude (thicker air) improves performance, while higher density altitude (thinner air) reduces lift, climb performance, propeller efficiency, and battery endurance. High density altitude is not your true altitude; it is how the air behaves for the aircraft.

sub_units.title: Air Pressure and Density Fundamentals
sub_units.description: Define air pressure and air density, identify standard sea-level atmospheric values, and explain why pressure and density decrease with altitude.
sub_units.text_content:
Air pressure is the weight of the air above you caused by gravity. More air above you produces higher pressure, and less air above you produces lower pressure. As altitude increases, pressure decreases.

Pressure can be reported in inches of mercury (inHg) or millibars (mb). Standard sea-level atmosphere references are 29.92 inHg (1,013.2 mb) and 59°F (15°C).

Air density is the amount of air molecules in a given volume. As altitude increases and pressure drops, density also drops. High-density air (thick air) improves lift and propeller efficiency; low-density air (thin air) reduces lift and thrust.

sub_units.title: Density Altitude and Performance
sub_units.description: Define pressure altitude and density altitude and apply the high-hot-humid concept to predict reductions in sUAS lift, climb, propeller efficiency, and battery endurance.
sub_units.text_content:
Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature; it is the altitude in the standard atmosphere that corresponds to the current air density. Pressure altitude is the altitude shown when the altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg.

Standard temperature is 15°C at sea level and decreases about 2°C (3.5°F) per 1,000 feet. If actual temperature is above standard, density altitude increases.

High altitude, high temperature, and high humidity all reduce density. Exam mindset: mountains, summer heat, and humid conditions all point to lower density and higher density altitude.

Operational effect: high density altitude means reduced lift, reduced climb capability, reduced propeller efficiency, and shorter battery endurance. Lower density altitude means better performance.

sub_units.title: Pressure, Humidity, and Performance
sub_units.description: Explain how pressure and humidity influence density and weather behavior, including compressibility, relative humidity, and why pressure changes with altitude are usually the dominant density driver.
sub_units.text_content:
Pressure and density are linked because air is a compressible fluid. Increasing pressure compresses a given volume of air and raises density; decreasing pressure allows expansion and lowers density. Pressure can change by changing the number of molecules in a volume or by changing molecular energy (temperature). In the real atmosphere, both temperature and pressure usually decrease with altitude, but pressure typically decreases faster, so density usually decreases with altitude.

Pressure patterns also signal broad weather behavior. Low pressure often supports rising air, cooling, cloud formation, and less stable weather. High pressure usually supports sinking air, warming, and generally clearer, more stable conditions.

Humidity is the amount of water vapor in air. Relative humidity is the percentage of water vapor present compared with the maximum the air can hold at that temperature. Warm air can hold more water vapor than cold air. Dry air is 0% relative humidity; saturated air is 100%; 50% relative humidity means the air contains half of the moisture it could hold at that temperature.

Water vapor is lighter than the main dry-air gases, so replacing oxygen and nitrogen with more water vapor lowers density. Practical result: higher humidity lowers air density and performance, and contributes to higher density altitude.

sub_units.title: Wind, Turbulence, and Severe Wind Hazards
sub_units.description: Explain how terrain, structures, open terrain, and weather systems create turbulence, wind shear, and microbursts, and apply operational precautions to avoid exceeding aircraft limits.
sub_units.text_content:
Wind effects depend on obstacles, terrain, and weather systems. Turbulence increases with obstacle size and wind speed. Expect turbulence around buildings, hangars, tree lines, bluffs, canyons, and mountains. Wind around corners and over rooftops can create strong mechanical turbulence on the leeward side where airflow separates. These disturbances can degrade aircraft stability and also reduce image quality for video and photography missions.

Use extra caution near open fields and water. With fewer obstacles to slow it, wind may be stronger than in nearby sheltered areas, and gusts may exceed manufacturer limits. If observed or forecast wind exceeds aircraft limits, do not fly.

In mountainous terrain, airflow is often smoother on the windward side and more turbulent on the leeward side, where descending and rotating flow can force an aircraft toward terrain.

sub_units.title: Obstacle and Terrain-Induced Turbulence
sub_units.description: Identify terrain and structure-driven turbulence patterns, including windward versus leeward mountain flow and building-corner turbulence, and apply safe operating cautions.
sub_units.text_content:
Obstacles create turbulence, and turbulence strength depends on obstacle size and wind velocity. Expect disturbed air around structures, tree lines, mountains, bluffs, and canyons.

Mountain terrain often provides smoother lifting flow on the windward side, but increasingly turbulent descending flow on the leeward side that can push an aircraft toward terrain.

Around buildings, wind often cannot follow sharp corners cleanly and separates, creating turbulent eddies and gusts on the leeward side.

sub_units.title: Low-Level Wind Shear and Microbursts
sub_units.description: Define wind shear and microbursts, identify common triggers, and explain why these hazards are especially dangerous for low-altitude sUAS operations.
sub_units.text_content:
Wind shear is a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance. It can occur at any altitude and may create updrafts, downdrafts, and abrupt horizontal wind shifts. It is especially dangerous close to the ground where recovery margins are small.

Wind shear is associated with frontal systems, thunderstorms, temperature inversions, and strong upper-level winds (often greater than 25 knots).

Microbursts are intense localized downdrafts from convective weather that spread outward at the surface and can force small aircraft downward rapidly.

sub_units.title: Operational Wind Risk Decisions
sub_units.description: Apply practical go/no-go decisions for wind by comparing observed and forecast conditions with manufacturer limits, terrain effects, and mission requirements.
sub_units.text_content: Before launch, compare forecast and observed wind and gust values to manufacturer limits. Reassess at the site because local terrain and structures can make actual winds much stronger than regional reports. When operating near mountains, ridges, buildings, or shoreline/open-field transitions, expect changing wind direction and turbulence. If conditions approach limits, reduce exposure, increase standoff from obstacles, or delay/cancel the mission.

sub_units.title: Atmospheric Stability and Temperature Inversions
sub_units.description: Explain atmospheric stability, convective motion, and temperature inversions, including their effects on turbulence, cloud development, trapped pollutants, and visibility.
sub_units.text_content:
Atmospheric stability describes how strongly the air resists vertical movement. Uneven surface heating produces convective currents: warm air rises (updrafts) and cool air sinks (downdrafts). Unstable air supports stronger vertical motion, turbulence, and vertically developed cumuliform clouds. Cool, dry air tends to be more stable and generally supports clearer weather. Stratiform clouds are generally layered/horizontal and associated with more stable conditions, while cumuliform clouds are vertically developed and associated with instability.

Normally, air temperature decreases with altitude. A temperature inversion exists when temperature increases with altitude. Inversions are often shallow, smooth, and stable near the surface and can act like a lid that traps moisture, haze, smoke, and other pollutants below the inversion layer. Surface-based inversions commonly form on clear, cool nights; frontal inversions occur where warm air overruns colder air or colder air wedges beneath warmer air. High humidity within an inversion can reduce visibility and support fog/haze/smog development.

sub_units.title: Atmospheric Stability and Convective Currents
sub_units.description: Describe how moisture and temperature determine stability and connect unstable versus stable air to convective currents, turbulence, and cloud type.
sub_units.text_content:
Convective currents form when uneven heating causes warm air to rise and cooler air to sink. Instability allows vertical motions to strengthen, increasing turbulence and supporting vertical cloud growth.

Warm, moist air often supports unstable conditions and cumuliform cloud development. Cool, dry air tends to be stable and resists vertical motion, favoring layered (stratiform) clouds and generally smoother conditions.

sub_units.title: Temperature Inversions
sub_units.description: Define temperature inversions, contrast them with the normal lapse rate, and explain how surface and frontal inversions affect turbulence and visibility.
sub_units.text_content:
Under normal conditions, temperature decreases with altitude. In a temperature inversion, temperature increases with altitude.

Inversions often create smooth, stable layers near the surface and act as a lid that traps moisture and pollutants. Surface inversions commonly form on clear, cool nights as the ground cools nearby air. Frontal inversions occur when warm air overlies cooler air or cooler air undercuts warmer air.

When humidity is high, inversion layers can worsen visibility through fog, haze, smoke, and smog.

sub_units.title: Dew Point and Structural Icing Hazards
sub_units.description: Explain the temperature–dew point relationship, condensation effects on aircraft surfaces, and the two required conditions for structural icing, including aerodynamic cooling risk near freezing.
sub_units.text_content:
Dew point is the temperature at which air becomes saturated and can no longer hold additional water vapor. The spread between air temperature and dew point helps describe relative humidity; as temperature approaches dew point, relative humidity rises. When air cools to its dew point, condensation forms as fog, dew, frost, cloud, rain, or snow depending on conditions.

Moisture on aircraft surfaces can reduce performance by adding weight and disturbing airflow.

Structural icing is ice accumulation on aircraft surfaces such as wings, body surfaces, and propellers. Two conditions are required in flight: visible moisture (for example cloud droplets or rain) and a surface temperature at or below 0°C where moisture impacts. Aerodynamic cooling can lower local surface temperature to freezing even when ambient air is slightly above 0°C, so icing can still occur near-freezing conditions.

sub_units.title: Dew Point and Condensation
sub_units.description: Define dew point and explain how temperature–dew point spread relates to relative humidity and condensation products that can degrade sUAS performance.
sub_units.text_content:
Dew point is the temperature at which air is saturated. The closer air temperature is to dew point, the higher the relative humidity.

When temperature cools to dew point, moisture condenses into fog, dew, frost, clouds, rain, or snow depending on conditions. Dew or frost on aircraft surfaces adds weight and disrupts airflow, reducing lift and performance.

sub_units.title: Structural Icing
sub_units.description: Identify structural icing hazards, the two required icing conditions, and why aerodynamic cooling makes near-freezing operations risky.
sub_units.text_content:
Structural icing is dangerous because ice increases drag, reduces lift, and adds weight.

Two conditions are required for structural icing in flight: the aircraft must be in visible moisture, and the surface where moisture strikes must be 0°C or colder.

Aerodynamic cooling can reduce local surface temperature below ambient, allowing icing even when outside air temperature is slightly above freezing.

sub_units.title: Clouds and Thunderstorm Life Cycle
sub_units.description: Identify hazardous cloud types and fully describe the thunderstorm life cycle, including stage-specific hazards such as turbulence, wind shear, hail, lightning, and microbursts.
sub_units.text_content:
Cumuliform clouds are puffy clouds with vertical development that can grow into cumulonimbus thunderstorms. Cumulonimbus clouds are among the most hazardous cloud types for small UAS because of strong convection, severe turbulence, lightning, hail, heavy precipitation, and possible tornadoes. Small UAS can encounter updrafts and downdrafts that exceed 3,000 feet per minute in convective conditions.

Standing lenticular altocumulus clouds form in mountain-wave flow and often indicate strong winds and turbulence, especially on the lee side of terrain. They can appear smooth with little visible movement even while dangerous winds persist.

Thunderstorms progress through cumulus, mature, and dissipating stages, and multicell storms may contain different stages at the same time.

sub_units.title: Hazardous Cloud Types
sub_units.description: Recognize cumuliform, cumulonimbus, and standing lenticular clouds and explain the specific turbulence and severe-weather hazards each signals for sUAS operations.
sub_units.text_content:
Cumuliform clouds indicate vertical development and potential convective turbulence. Cumulonimbus clouds are highly hazardous, with severe turbulence, lightning, hail, heavy precipitation, and possible tornado activity.

Standing lenticular altocumulus clouds form in mountain waves, often have smooth polished edges, and can appear nearly stationary even while strong winds and severe lee-side turbulence are present.

sub_units.title: Thunderstorm Life Cycle
sub_units.description: Explain cumulonimbus storm evolution through cumulus, mature, and dissipating stages and identify the key hazard cues in each stage for go/no-go decisions.
sub_units.text_content:
Thunderstorms evolve through three stages:

Cumulus stage: dominated by strong updrafts and rapid vertical growth (often greater than 3,000 ft/min). Water droplets grow as rising air carries moisture upward; icing risk can develop aloft where supercooled liquid water exists.

Mature stage: begins when precipitation starts and strong downdrafts develop alongside updrafts. This is the most dangerous phase, with severe turbulence, vertical wind shear, hail, lightning, microbursts, and strong gusty outflow at the surface (including the first gust/plow-wind surge), often with rapid temperature drops and pressure rises near outflow boundaries.

Dissipating stage: downdrafts dominate and then weaken. Precipitation decreases and the cell breaks apart; once downdrafts cease, the storm cell has dissipated.

sub_units.title: Fronts, Mountain Flying, and Operational Minimums
sub_units.description: Explain warm and cold front behavior, apply mountain-weather cautions, and use ceiling and visibility information to comply with Part 107 minimums and make safe flight decisions.
sub_units.text_content:
A front is the boundary between unlike air masses where temperature, humidity, pressure trend, and wind can change rapidly. Warm fronts generally advance more slowly and can provide earlier warning as conditions deteriorate over longer periods. Cold fronts are typically faster and can bring abrupt weather changes with less warning.

Mountain weather requires careful preflight planning with cloud reports, wind direction, wind speed, and stability information. Winds at mountaintop level can differ significantly from winds partway up the slope. Windward sides may be comparatively smoother, while lee sides can contain severe turbulence, rotor effects, and descending flow. Standing lenticular and rotor cloud indicators require extra caution.

Operational minimums rely on ceiling and visibility awareness. Ceiling is the lowest broken or overcast cloud layer, or vertical visibility into an obscuration. Visibility is the greatest horizontal distance prominent objects can be seen with unaided vision.

sub_units.title: Atmospheric Fronts
sub_units.description: Define frontal zones and compare warm versus cold fronts using movement speed, pressure trends, and likely operational weather impacts.
sub_units.text_content:
A frontal zone is the transition area between unlike air masses where weather variables can change quickly over short distances.

Warm front: warm air advances over cooler air, often with broader and longer-duration weather changes; pressure typically falls as the front approaches and improves after passage.

Cold front: colder, denser air undercuts warmer air and typically advances faster with sharper weather changes. A high dew point with falling barometric pressure can indicate approaching frontal weather and rising convective risk.

sub_units.title: Mountain Flying Cautions
sub_units.description: Apply mountain-flight weather planning by interpreting cloud and wind indicators, including lee-side turbulence, rotor/lenticular cues, and wind variation with elevation.
sub_units.text_content:
Before mountain operations, gather cloud reports, wind direction, wind speed, and stability information, then maintain continuous situational awareness because complete information may not always be available.

Wind can differ substantially between mountaintop level and locations partway up the mountain. Stratiform clouds generally indicate more stable air. Convective clouds on windward slopes indicate instability and increased turbulence potential. Standing lenticular and rotor-cloud indicators suggest strong lee-side turbulence, while windward flow may be relatively smoother.

sub_units.title: Operational Ceiling and Visibility Minimums
sub_units.description: Define ceiling and visibility reporting terms and apply Part 107 cloud-clearance and visibility requirements using METAR/automated observations and forecast briefings.
sub_units.text_content:
Ceiling is the lowest cloud layer reported as broken (5/8 to 7/8 coverage) or overcast (8/8 coverage), or vertical visibility into obscurations such as fog or haze. Ceiling is reported in METAR observations and automated weather reports.

Visibility is the greatest horizontal distance at which prominent objects can be seen with unaided vision. Current and forecast visibility come from METAR/automated systems and official weather briefings.

Part 107 weather minimums include at least 3 statute miles visibility and required cloud clearance of at least 500 feet below and 2,000 feet horizontally from clouds.

units.title: LOADING AND PERFORMANCE
units.description: By the end of this unit, you will understand a comprehensive breakdown of the forces acting on an aircraft during flight, critical weight and balance considerations for the Remote Pilot in Command (RPIC), the concept and effects of load factors, and how these factors relate to stalling speed and overall flight safety.
units.text_content: The unit covers the four forces in flight (Lift, Gravity, Thrust, Drag), and how those forces are affected during a turn, resulting in increased load factor. It details the RPIC's pre-flight responsibilities regarding weight and balance, defining components like Center of Gravity (CG) and Center of Pressure (CP) and their relationship. A major focus is on load factors, which are measured in Gs and increase structural stress and stalling speed, especially beyond 45° of bank. Finally, the unit explores the negative impacts of excessive weight on performance and safety considerations, particularly in high-altitude/high-temperature conditions.

sub_units.title: FORCES IN FLIGHT
sub_units.text_content: In straight & level flight, four forces always act on an aircraft: Lift, Gravity, Thrust, Drag. These forces affect fixed‑wing aircraft and multirotor differently, but the basic principles are the same for all aircraft.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on FORCES IN FLIGHT, you will describes the four forces that always act on an aircraft in straight and level flight, noting that the basic principles apply to both fixed-wing aircraft and multirotors.

sub_units.title: FORCES DURING A TURN
sub_units.description: Explain ow lift is divided during a turn, the introduction of centrifugal force, and the resulting increase in load factor and stress on the aircraft structure.
sub_units.text_content: When an aircraft turns, lift is split into two components: a vertical component that opposes gravity & a horizontal component that makes the aircraft turn. During the turn, the aircraft also experiences centrifugal force, pulling it outward from the center of the turn. This force combines with gravity, increasing the load factor & stress on the aircraft. Pilots must understand how these increased forces affect aircraft performance & adjust their control inputs accordingly.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT & BALANCE CONSIDERATIONS FOR RPIC
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on WEIGHT & BALANCE CONSIDERATIONS FOR RPIC, you will lists the pre-flight responsibilities of the Remote Pilot in Command (RPIC) regarding aircraft weight and balance to ensure flight safety.
sub_units.text_content:
Pre-Flight Responsibilities:
- Verify aircraft is correctly loaded.
- Determine weight and balance condition of the aircraft.
- Follow manufacturer/builder restrictions for weight and balance.
- Ensure compliance with limits for flight safety.
- Consider consequences of overweight aircraft in emergencies.

sub_units.title: LOAD
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on LOAD, you will outlines the essential understanding of load and respecting operating limitations required for Part 107 pilots.
sub_units.text_content:
Overview: Outlines the essential understanding of load and respecting operating limitations required for Part 107 pilots.

Don’t need advanced engineering degrees as part of the Part 107 but, pilots should:
- Understand forces acting on the aircraft.
- Know how to use these forces advantageously.
- Respect operating limitations of the aircraft.

sub_units.title: MAXIMUM GROSS TAKEOFF WEIGHT
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on MAXIMUM GROSS TAKEOFF WEIGHT, you will discusses the manufacturer-specified maximum takeoff weight and the environmental and operational conditions (including the '3 H' factors) that necessitate a weight reduction.
sub_units.text_content:
Maximum takeoff weight specified by manufacturer but may not guarantee safe takeoff under all conditions.
Conditions requiring weight reduction (3 H):
- High elevation
- High temperature
- High humidity
Other conditions requiring reduction in weight:
- Runway/launch area length
- Surface type and slope
- Surface wind
- Obstacles present

sub_units.title: WEIGHT CHANGES DURING FLIGHT
sub_units.description: Explain ow weight changes during flight, including fuel burn in general aviation and changes from expendable items or decreasing battery power in small UAS operations.
sub_units.text_content:
Fuel burn:
- Aircraft becomes lighter → performance improves.
- May negatively affect balance.
Small UA operations:
- Weight change from expendable items (e.g., jettisonable load).
- As battery power goes down the ability to produce lift could decrease.

sub_units.title: BALANCE AND CENTER OF GRAVITY (CG)
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on BALANCE AND CENTER OF GRAVITY (CG), you will introduces the concept of adverse balance conditions and defines the two key components of balance: Center of Gravity (CG) and Center of Pressure (CP).
sub_units.text_content:
Adverse balance conditions affect flight like excess weight.
Balance is made up of two key components:
- Center of Gravity
- Center of Pressure
Manufacturer sets Center of Gravity (CG) limits.

sub_units.title: BALANCE AND CENTER OF GRAVITY (CG) - RPIC Management
sub_units.description: Explain at the Center of Gravity (CG) is dynamic and outlines the RPIC's responsibility to manage CG shifts to ensure it remains within allowable limits, especially when adding payloads.
sub_units.text_content:
CG is not fixed; depends on weight distribution.
Position shifts as loads move or are used, ex: luggage, fuel or people.
RPIC must:
- Determine CG shift and its effect.
- Ensure CG remains within allowable limits or relocate or shed weight before flight.
- If adding additional cameras or sensors – need to check proper loading.

sub_units.title: GRAVITY
sub_units.description: Define and recognize gravity and the Center of Gravity (CG), explaining the CG as the focal point of aircraft weight and its role in balancing.
sub_units.text_content:
Gravity - Pulling force that draws bodies toward Earth’s center.
Aircraft weight acts through the Center of Gravity (CG).
CG - Point where all aircraft weight is focused.
- If supported at CG → aircraft balances in any attitude.
CG Location:
- Determined by aircraft design.
- Designers account for center of pressure (CP) travel.

sub_units.title: CG VS. CP RELATIONSHIP - Stability Concept
sub_units.description: Define and recognize the Center of Pressure (CP) and explains the stability implications, noting that for stability, the CG must be positioned ahead of the CP.
sub_units.text_content:
CP - Center of lift, the opposite of CG.
The aerodynamic forces of lift occur at the CP.
An aircraft’s weight is concentrated at the CG.
- CG forward of CP → Nose-down tendency.
- CP forward of CG → Nose-up tendency.
Designers make the rear-most allowable position of the center of gravity (CG) ahead of the center of pressure (CP) for stability.

sub_units.title: CG VS. CP RELATIONSHIP - Aircraft Type
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on CG VS. CP RELATIONSHIP - Aircraft Type, you will compares the typical locations of the Center of Gravity (CG) and Center of Pressure (CP) on multi-rotor versus fixed-wing aircraft.
sub_units.text_content:
CP and CG on traditionally designed multi-rotors are in the center of the aircraft.
On fixed-wing aircraft, the CP it is about 1/3 back from the leading edge of the wing.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT AND LIFT RELATIONSHIP
sub_units.description: Explain direct relationship between aircraft weight and lift, defining lift and describing the three equilibrium states (level flight, descent, climb).
sub_units.text_content:
The weight of an aircraft has a direct relationship to the lift generated by its wings or propellers.
Lift:
- Upward force on wing.
- Acts perpendicular to relative wind and aircraft’s lateral axis.
Lift vs. Weight:
- Level flight: Lift = Weight → Equilibrium
- Lift < Weight → aircraft descends
- Lift > Weight → aircraft climbs

sub_units.title: STABILITY
sub_units.description: Define and recognize aircraft stability as the design characteristic that allows it to return to equilibrium after a disturbance.
sub_units.text_content: Stability – The ability of an aircraft to correct for disturbances and return to or maintain its original flight path. Stability is primarily an aircraft design characteristic.

sub_units.title: STABILITY, MANEUVERABILITY & CONTROLLABILITY
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on STABILITY, MANEUVERABILITY & CONTROLLABILITY, you will differentiates and defines the three flight characteristics: stability, maneuverability, and controllability, listing the factors that influence each.
sub_units.text_content:
Stability – tendency to return to equilibrium.
Maneuverability - Ability to be manipulated easily & handle stress
- Influenced by: Aircraft weight, inertia, size and location of flight controls, and structural strength.
Controllability:
- Aircraft’s ability to respond to pilot input.
- Concerns flight path and attitude.
- A design characteristic – includes the ability to react to up & downdrafts or horizontal winds.

sub_units.title: LOAD FACTORS
sub_units.description: Define and recognize load factors and how they are measured in Gs (gravity units), explaining their trigonometric relationship to lift and weight.
sub_units.text_content:
Measured in Gs (acceleration of gravity):
- 1 G = force of gravity on a body at rest.
- Indicates force experienced when accelerated.
Load Factor – the maximum load factor (at given bank angle) is a proportion between lift and weight and has a trigonometric relationship.

sub_units.title: LOAD - Turn Stress
sub_units.description: Explain at load factor is the stress on the structure created by forces that deflect the aircraft, providing a definition and G-force examples.
sub_units.text_content:
When an aircraft goes into a turn it experiences a load factor determined by its lift required & angle of its turn.
Any force that deflects an aircraft from a straight path creates stress on its structure.
Load Factor = Amount of this force.
Example:
- Load factor of 3 → aircraft structure supports 3 times its weight.
- Expressed as 3 Gs, 4 Gs, etc.
- You experience more than 1G on your favorite ride.

sub_units.title: LOAD FACTORS - Pilot Concerns
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on LOAD FACTORS - Pilot Concerns, you will identifies the two critical safety reasons why pilots must be aware of load factors: the potential for structural overload and the increase in stalling speed.
sub_units.text_content:
Two critical reasons for pilots:
- Dangerous overload can be imposed on aircraft structures.
- Increased load factor raises stalling speed, making stalls possible even at what one would consider safe speeds.
Aircraft are designed to withstand only a certain amount of overload.

sub_units.title: LOAD FACTOR IN STEEP TURNS
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on LOAD FACTOR IN STEEP TURNS, you will identifies the two forces — centrifugal force and weight — that contribute to the load factor during a coordinated, constant-altitude steep turn.
sub_units.text_content:
Forces in a coordinated turn at constant altitude, load factor results from:
- Centrifugal force (outward force during turn)
- Weight (gravity pulling downward)

sub_units.title: RATE OF TURN (ROT) AND AIRSPEED
sub_units.description: Explain inverse relationship between airspeed and rate of turn (ROT) for a constant bank angle, and that this relationship keeps the load factor constant.
sub_units.text_content:
For a given bank angle:
- Higher airspeed → slower rate of turn
- This compensates for added centrifugal force, keeping load factor constant.

sub_units.title: LOAD FACTOR VS BANK ANGLE - Rapid Increase
sub_units.description: Describe the non-linear relationship between bank angle and load factor, noting the significant increase beyond 45° and the corresponding increase in required lift and stall speed.
sub_units.text_content:
Load factor increases rapidly beyond a 45°–50° bank, and stall speed increases significantly.
Examples:
- 60° bank → 2 Gs
- 80° bank → 5.76 Gs
Wing must produce lift equal to these load factors to maintain altitude.
This chart is in the testing supplement you will have during the test. The wing must produce lift equal to these load factors if altitude is to be maintained.

sub_units.title: LOAD FACTOR VS BANK ANGLE - Calculation Example
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on LOAD FACTOR VS BANK ANGLE - Calculation Example, you will provides a specific calculation example illustrating the load increase on a 20 lb Unmanned Aircraft (UA) during a 30-degree banked turn.
sub_units.text_content:
Overview: Provides a specific calculation example illustrating the load increase on a 20 lb Unmanned Aircraft (UA) during a 30-degree banked turn.

If a UA weighs 20 lbs, the approximate weight the UA would be required to support during a 30-degree banked turn while maintaining altitude would be: 20 x 1.154 = 23 lbs.

sub_units.title: EXTREME BANK ANGLES
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on EXTREME BANK ANGLES, you will discusses the physical limitations of extreme bank angles, noting that a constant-altitude turn is impossible at 90° and that load factors exceed acrobatic limits above 80°.
sub_units.text_content:
Overview: Discusses the physical limitations of extreme bank angles, noting that a constant-altitude turn is impossible at 90° and that load factors exceed acrobatic limits above 80°.

At 90° bank, constant-altitude turn is not possible.
Aircraft can bank 90° in a slipping turn if not holding altitude.
At slightly more than 80°, load factor exceeds 6Gs (limit for acrobatic aircraft).

sub_units.title: STALLS AND LOAD FACTOR - Stall Definition
sub_units.description: Define and recognize a stall, explaining that it can occur at any airspeed when the Angle of Attack (AOA) is too high, leading to airflow separation and lift loss.
sub_units.text_content:
Any aircraft can stall at any airspeed within structural limits.
Occurs when: Angle of Attack (AOA) becomes too high and the smooth airflow over the wing breaks up and separates.
Resulting in:
- Abrupt change in flight characteristics
- Sudden loss of lift → stall

sub_units.title: STALLING SPEED & LOAD FACTOR
sub_units.description: Explain proportional relationship between stalling speed and load factor (proportional to the square root of the load factor) and provides a quantitative example.
sub_units.text_content:
Stalling speed increases with load factor - Proportional to the √ of the load factor.
Ex: An aircraft with a normal unaccelerated stalling speed of 50 knots can be stalled at 100 knots by inducing a load factor of 4Gs.
Load factors are measured in Gs (acceleration of gravity) – If a UAV has 4Gs, the total load on a UAVs structure is 4x its actual weight.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT AND BALANCE - Limits and Consequences
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on WEIGHT AND BALANCE - Limits and Consequences, you will reiterates the critical nature of weight and balance limits and the negative consequences of exceeding maximum weight or allowing the Center of Gravity (CG) to shift out of bounds.
sub_units.text_content:
Weight and balance limits are critical for flight safety.
Exceeding maximum weight can:
- Compromise structural integrity.
- Reduce overall aircraft performance.
Center of gravity (CG) → outside allowable limits → results in:
- Control difficulty
- Possible loss of aircraft stability.
RPIC responsible for reviewing aircraft weight & balance data, especially for drones with payloads.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT - Definition
sub_units.description: Define and recognize weight as a downward force of gravity determined by mass and acceleration, which must be counteracted by lift.
sub_units.text_content:
Weight = force of gravity acting on an object.
Determined by:
- The object’s mass
- Earth’s gravitational acceleration
Weight is constantly acting downward and must be counteracted by lift.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT - Lift Dependence
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on WEIGHT - Lift Dependence, you will lists the four factors that lift depends on: airfoil design, Angle of Attack (AOA), airspeed, and air density, and the consequence of weight exceeding available lift.
sub_units.text_content:
Amount of lift depends on:
- Airfoil design
- Angle of attack (AOA)
- Airspeed
- Air density
If aircraft weight exceeds available lift → flight may be impossible.

sub_units.title: WEIGHT - Excessive Weight Degradations
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on WEIGHT - Excessive Weight Degradations, you will lists the specific and comprehensive performance degradations caused by operating with excessive weight.
sub_units.text_content:
Excessive weight negatively impacts many aspects of flight including failing to take off.
Key Performance Degradations:
- Higher takeoff speed required
- Longer takeoff distance
- Reduced rate & angle of climb
- Lower maximum altitude
- Shorter flight range
- Reduced cruising speed
- Reduced maneuverability
- Higher stall speed
- Higher approach & landing speeds
- Longer landing distance

sub_units.title: SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS, you will highlights conditions (high altitude, high density altitude, strong winds, high temperatures) that compound the dangers of overloading, and notes the impact on a pilot's emergency response capability.
sub_units.text_content:
Overloading becomes even more dangerous when combined with:
- High altitude
- High density altitude
- Strong winds
- High temperatures
In emergencies excessive weight can reduce the pilot’s ability to respond effectively.

sub_units.title: KEY TAKEAWAYS
sub_units.description: Summarize the essential points regarding load factor, Gs, stall speed, structural stress, and the role of bank angle.
sub_units.text_content:
Load factor = stress on aircraft structure during turn.
Measured in Gs (gravity units).
Higher load factor → higher stall speed and greater structural stress.
Bank angle increases load factor significantly.

sub_units.title: QUESTIONS AND COMMENTS
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on QUESTIONS AND COMMENTS, you will directs users to post questions or comments.
sub_units.text_content: Post any questions or comments in the post.

units.title: EMERGENCY PROCEDURES
units.description: By the end of this lesson on EMERGENCY PROCEDURES, you will this unit outlines how to prepare for and handle unexpected in-flight emergencies, covering the Remote Pilot in Command's (RPIC) responsibilities for planning, crew briefing, regulatory deviation authority, and required post-emergency documentation.
units.text_content:
Overview: This unit outlines how to prepare for and handle unexpected in-flight emergencies, covering the Remote Pilot in Command's (RPIC) responsibilities for planning, crew briefing, regulatory deviation authority, and required post-emergency documentation.

Introduction to Emergency Procedures
An in‑flight emergency is an unexpected event that can create serious risks for an unprepared remote pilot. The RPIC is responsible for safe operation of the Unmanned Aircraft (UA) at all times & should plan for emergencies in the preflight planning process.

sub_units.title: Potential Hazards and Accident Causes
sub_units.text_content:
Some examples of unexpected events that could cause accidents are:
- Unexpected Manned Aircraft Entering the Area
- Propeller or Motor Malfunction
- Wildlife Interference
- Battery Depletion Faster Than Expected
- Unexpected Obstacles
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Potential Hazards and Accident Causes, you will a list of common in-flight emergencies and unexpected events that the RPIC must plan for.

sub_units.title: Preflight Planning, Checklists, and Crew Briefing
sub_units.text_content:
Preparation and Crew Management
- Checklists: Some manufacturers provide preflight checklists for safe operations. If no manufacturer checklist exists, the remote pilot must create one to ensure safety.
- Crew Briefing: All crew members must be briefed on operational details and emergency procedures. Having an emergency plan that everyone has been briefed on enables the crew to remain calm, as training will kick in. When additional crew members are used, they must all be briefed before the flight.
sub_units.description: Explain Requirements for preflight checklists, and the procedure and necessity of briefing all crew members on operational details and emergency plans.

sub_units.title: Regulatory Deviation and Documentation
sub_units.text_content:
Overview: Explaining the remote pilot's authority to break Part 107 rules for safety during an emergency and the requirements for post-emergency action and reporting.

Authority to Deviate
- Deviation: During an emergency, a remote pilot may deviate from any Part 107 regulation if necessary for safety.
- Return to Normal: Once the emergency has ended, the remote pilot must return to normal Part 107 operating regulations.
- Documentation: Document what happened once you return to safety, including videos or pictures.
- Reporting: When rules are broken for safety, reporting to the FAA is only required when asked.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Regulatory Deviation and Documentation, you will explaining the remote pilot's authority to break Part 107 rules for safety during an emergency and the requirements for post-emergency action and reporting.

sub_units.title: Example Emergency Scenario
sub_units.text_content:
Example: Manned Aircraft Hazard
- Scenario: Creating a video at a beach; weather is clear and the area is low-risk. You complete preflight tasks, establish your takeoff/landing zone, and brief your Visual Observer (VO).
- Hazard Detected (Before Launch): You notice a helicopter flying low where you were planning to fly.
- Required Action: Since you haven’t launched, keep the UA grounded, maintain visuals of the helicopter, communicate with the crew, reassess the situation after the aircraft passes, update your risk assessment before flying, and launch when completely clear.
- Action (If Airborne): If you were already airborne, you would make decisions to avoid causing an in‑flight emergency.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson on Example Emergency Scenario, you will a step-by-step procedure for handling a common pre-flight hazard involving manned aircraft.

units.title: AERONAUTICAL DECISION MAKING
units.description: By the end of this unit, you will explain Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM) and apply risk management, hazardous attitude antidotes, CRM/SRM, physiology and vision factors, and maintenance responsibilities to safer sUAS operations.
units.text_content:
Overview: This unit details Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM), a systematic process for safe flight, covering risk management principles, hazardous attitudes and antidotes, resource management (CRM and SRM), physiological factors affecting pilots, vision scanning techniques, and required sUAS maintenance and inspection procedures.

Overview of Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)

ADM is a systematic process used by pilots to determine the best course of action in response to a specific situation. It helps pilots make consistent, safe, and informed decisions, based on the most current information available, providing feedback to pilots. ADM is essential to safe flight operations because human error is the leading cause of aviation accidents.

### Risk Management and Flight Operations

Risks are present in everything we do. The goal of Risk Management is to proactively identify safety‑related hazards before they happen and mitigate or remove the risks. Risk Management should be integrated into all phases of flight—before, during, and after. Post-flight, an After Action Review (AAR) should be conducted after each job to discuss successes, failures, and lessons learned for the next time.

### Pilot Fitness and Safety Checks

Part 107 explicitly prohibits drone operation if the Remote PIC, person controlling the drone, or Visual Observer cannot perform their duties safely. The RPIC must ensure all crewmembers are not impaired during the operation. To mitigate risks related to human factors, pilots use checklists like IMSAFE and PAVE to assess fitness, environmental conditions, and external pressures.

### Maintenance and Inspection

Part 107 (14 CFR) requires all sUAS operators to ensure their drone is safe for operation. This includes scheduled and unscheduled maintenance, repair, inspection, modification, replacement, and system software upgrades of the sUAS and all of its components, which must be performed according to manufacturer’s instructions.

sub_units.title: Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)
sub_units.text_content:
Definition and Importance

   ADM is a systematic process used by pilots to determine the best course of action in response to a specific situation.
   It helps pilots make consistent, safe, and informed decisions.
   Decisions are based on the most current information available to the pilot, providing feedback.
   Human error is the leading cause of aviation accidents.
*   ADM is essential to safe flight operations.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will explain what ADM is, why it is systematic, and why it is critical to reducing human error in aviation.

sub_units.title: Steps to Good Decision Making (Part 1)
sub_units.text_content: 1. Identify personal attitudes hazardous to safe flight\n   2. Learn behavior modification techniques - Replace hazardous attitudes with safer, more accurate thinking.\n*   3. Learn how to recognize and deal with stress
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will apply the first three ADM decision-making steps: identifying hazardous attitudes, using behavior modification, and recognizing and managing stress.

sub_units.title: Steps to Good Decision Making (Part 2)
sub_units.text_content:
4. Develop risk assessment skills - Evaluate hazards with tools like PAVE, CARE, and DECIDE models.
      - CARE: Consider consequences, Alternatives, Reality, and External pressures before choosing an action.
      - DECIDE: Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, and Evaluate.
   5. Use all resources.
*   6. Evaluate the effectiveness of one’s ADM skills - Does outcome support safety and mission goals?
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will apply the final three ADM decision-making steps: assessing risk with tools such as PAVE/CARE/DECIDE, using available resources, and evaluating ADM outcomes.

sub_units.title: Risk Management
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will describe risk management goals, decision foundations and principles, and continuous before/during/after flight risk review including AAR.

sub_units.title: Hazards and Risk Assessment
sub_units.text_content:
Hazard: A real or perceived condition, event, or circumstance that a pilot encounters.
   When faced with a hazard, the pilot makes an assessment based upon various factors.
   Different pilots see hazards differently.
   The pilot assigns a value to the potential impact of the hazard.
   Throughout the flight, the pilot will make many decisions under hazardous conditions.
   The pilot needs to assess the degree of risk and determine the best course of action to mitigate the risk.
   Severity Levels (Event Occurrence Likelihood):
       Probable - event will occur several times
       Occasional - event will probably occur sometime
       Remote - event is unlikely to occur, but possible
    *   Improbable - event is highly unlikely to occur
sub_units.description: Define a hazard and risk, detailing the pilot's role in assessing risk severity, and listing the four levels of event occurrence likelihood.

sub_units.title: Hazardous Attitudes and Antidotes
sub_units.text_content:
Hazardous attitudes can negatively affect pilot judgment, putting everyone at risk, but can be countered by redirecting those attitudes toward safer, more appropriate actions.
   Recognition of hazardous thoughts is the first step toward defusing them.
   After recognizing it as hazardous, the pilot should label it as hazardous, then state the corresponding antidote.
   Pilots should memorize the antidotes for each hazardous attitude for the test.
   Common hazardous attitudes and antidotes:
      - Anti-authority (“Don’t tell me”) -> Follow the rules; they are usually right.
      - Impulsivity (“Do something quickly”) -> Not so fast; think first.
      - Invulnerability (“It won’t happen to me”) -> It could happen to me.
      - Macho (“I can do it”) -> Taking chances is foolish.
      - Resignation (“What’s the use”) -> I can make a difference.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will recognize hazardous attitudes and apply the corresponding antidote mindset to support safer decisions.

sub_units.title: Mitigating Risks: IMSAFE Checklist
sub_units.text_content:
The IMSAFE checklist ensures everyone on the team is physically and mentally well to minimize the risk of the operation.
   If team members are not well, the team, equipment, and general public are put at risk.
   There is no test requirement for operators, so self-certification of fitness is required.
   Failure to self-assess could result in being tired or having an unclear head, making poor decisions as problems arise.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will use IMSAFE to self-assess crew physical and mental readiness before operations.

sub_units.title: Mitigating Risks: PAVE Checklist
sub_units.text_content:
The PAVE checklist helps pilots reduce risk by spotting hazards and managing risk before the flight.
   PAVE organizes hazards into four areas: Pilot, Aircraft, Environment, and External Pressures (Life, work pressures).
   The RPIC is responsible for deciding if it is safe to fly, identifying flight risks, and determining if risks can be managed safely and successfully.
   If proceeding with the flight, strategies must be developed to mitigate risks (e.g., setting personal minimums for each risk category).
*   Maintaining situational awareness helps prevent losing control; risks are dealt with by identifying the hazard, assessing its seriousness, and planning to reduce risk to an acceptable level (e.g., creating a checklist for future jobs).
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will apply the PAVE checklist and RPIC risk-mitigation responsibilities to preflight go/no-go decisions.

sub_units.text_content:
Principles of Risk Management

   Goal: Identify safety‑related hazards before they happen and mitigate or remove the risks.
   Foundation of Decisions: Decisions are based on a combination of Direct experience (e.g., seeing a driver get hurt from not wearing a seat belt), Indirect experience (e.g., seeing someone else injured from not wearing a seat belt), and Education and training (RPICs study accidents and lessons learned from others).
   Principles:
       Accept Unnecessary Risk – Is the outcome worth the risk?
       Make Risk Decisions at the Appropriate Level - usually, the RPIC.
       Integrate Risk Management Into All Phases of Flight – an ongoing process, not a one‑time step.
       Accept Risk When Benefits Outweigh the Costs.
   Process: An ongoing cycle where participants constantly reassess risks and evaluate whether their controls are reducing those risks.
   Application: Should be used during all aspects of the operation – before, during, and after.
   Post-Flight: Conduct an After Action Review (AAR) after each job to discuss what went right and wrong and what can be learned for the next time.

sub_units.title: Crew and Single-Pilot Resource Management
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will compare CRM and SRM and apply communication, workload, and situational awareness practices to improve operational safety.

sub_units.title: Effective Team Communication
sub_units.text_content:
Good communication among crew members is essential for a team.
   Methods: Use walkie-talkies, phones, or headsets to relay information, manage tasks effectively, and help prevent errors and accidents. Have a backup method of communication just in case.
   Knowing the phonetic alphabet helps eliminate communication errors.
   RPIC Role: The RPIC determines the communication method as part of preflight planning and communicates if changes are required.
   VO Role: The Visual Observer (VO) should communicate with the RPIC any hazards they see in the surrounding area while always maintaining visual contact with the drone.
   Ensure all team members are in the loop.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will explain crew communication methods, backup communication planning, and RPIC/VO communication responsibilities.

sub_units.title: Workload Management
sub_units.text_content: Definition: Planning team members, resources, and prioritizing tasks to get the important things done without becoming overloaded.\n   Best Practice: Do not overload team members—they must be able to concentrate on their tasks at hand. Work smarter, not harder.\n*   Technology: Technology (e.g., radio communications and weather apps) can help team members collect information, improving decision-making and situational awareness.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will apply workload management principles to prioritize tasks, avoid overload, and use technology to support safer decisions.

sub_units.title: Situational Awareness
sub_units.text_content:
Definition: Knowing what is happening around you during a flight.
   Involves understanding: Pilot, Aircraft, Environment, Operation, and the Overall flight situation.
   A pilot must know which factors are important and how they might affect the flight later.
   Focus: Do not focus too much on one single thing. Always know where the aircraft is and what is happening around it.
*   Threats: Fatigue, stress, and too much work can make it hard to stay aware. These issues can cause a pilot to fixate on one item and ignore everything else.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will define situational awareness, identify its key elements, and recognize common threats that degrade it.

sub_units.text_content:
Crew Resource Management (CRM)

   Definition: The effective use of all available resources, including Personnel, Equipment, Technical resources, and Information.
   Focus: Communication, leadership, decision making, situational awareness, and teamwork.
   Benefit: Ensures safe and efficient operations.
   Procedure: A formal procedure or Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) helps evaluate and mitigate operational risks associated with drone missions and aids the CRM process.

### Single-Pilot Resource Management (SRM)

   Definition: Gathering information, understanding it, and making good decisions.
   Tasks: Required before and during the flight.
   Concepts: Uses many of the CRM concepts.
   Goal: Teaches a pilot how to recognize problems, study the situation, and choose the best action.
   Requirement: Requires judgment, not just technique - THINK.
   Personalized Assessment: Every pilot must look at each situation based on their own experience, skill level, and personal minimums.
   Safety: Single pilots need to double-check, as there is no one else to do it.
   Decision Basis: A pilot must consider their physical condition and mental readiness before making a decision; the goal is to make the safest and most informed decision for that specific situation.

sub_units.title: Physiology and Vision
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will identify physiological and vision-related factors that affect safety and apply Part 107 impairment requirements to crew readiness.

sub_units.title: Vision and Scanning Techniques
sub_units.text_content:
Good vision skills make it easier to spot hazards in the sky.
   Pilots must learn proper scanning techniques to avoid missing important details.
   “See and avoid” relies on knowledge of the limitations of the human eye and the use of proper visual scanning techniques to compensate for these limitations.
   To scan effectively:
       Look from right to left or left to right.
       Begin scanning at the greatest distance an object can be perceived (top) and move inward.
       For each stop, scan an area approximately 30° wide.
       Stop (2 to 3 seconds) based on the degree of detail required.
    *   When moving from one viewing point to the next, overlap the previous field of view by 10°.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will explain the see-and-avoid concept and demonstrate proper visual scanning technique.

sub_units.title: Collision Threat Detection
sub_units.text_content:
Peripheral vision is helpful for noticing items that might be a collision threat.
   When a pilot pauses their scan and refocuses their eyes, peripheral vision helps detect movement.
   If you lose sight of your UA, make small movements left or right until you can see it again, or use a VO to scan specific sectors or the space.
   Movement is almost always the first awareness of a collision threat, and the discovery of a threat triggers your evasive action.
*   If another aircraft appears to have no relative motion, it is likely to be on a collision course with you; immediate evasive action should be taken.
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will explain collision threat cues and apply immediate evasive-action rules when relative motion indicates collision risk.

sub_units.text_content:
Overview: Overview of physiological factors, the Part 107 impairment rule, and specific health and environmental conditions pilots must be aware of.

Physiological Factors

   Part 107 does NOT allow drone operation if the Remote PIC, person controlling the drone, or Visual Observer cannot perform duties safely.
   Drone operators are exposed to additional elements not common in manned aviation.
   The RPIC must ensure all crewmembers are not impaired during the operation.
   Factors to be aware of include:
       Hypoxia - reduced oxygen
       Hyperventilation – can cause visual impairment, hot/cold, dizzy or tingling sensations, unconsciousness, and lightheaded
       Stress
       Fatigue
       Dehydration
       Heatstroke/Sunburn/Frostbite – dress appropriately, use a tent
    *   Effects of alcohol and drugs – Over-the-counter antihistamines and decongestants may cause drowsiness.

sub_units.title: Maintenance and Inspection Procedures
sub_units.description: By the end of this lesson, you will apply Part 107 maintenance and inspection requirements, including manufacturer guidance, self-developed programs, documentation, and RPIC accountability.
sub_units.text_content:
Overview: A comprehensive guide to sUAS maintenance and inspection, detailing Part 107 regulations, the scope of maintenance, the need for a self-developed program (if needed), and the RPIC's ultimate responsibility.

Part 107 Requirements

   (14 CFR) Part 107 requires all sUAS operators to make certain their drone is safe for operation.
   sUAS maintenance includes scheduled and unscheduled maintenance, repair, inspection, modification, replacement, and system software upgrades of the sUAS and all of its components.
   The operator should maintain the sUAS and its components in accordance with manufacturer’s instructions.
   The RPIC must verify the sUAS is safe prior to flying, even if someone else did the maintenance.
   Operators should complete the repairs based on the manufacture's recommendations.
   Some manufacturers require the person completing the repairs to be certified.

### Self-Developed Program

   Based on FAA guidance in AC 107‑2A, if the manufacturer does not provide a maintenance program, you should create a self-developed program.
   The program should cover:
       Scheduled inspections
       Replacement intervals (propellers, motors, batteries, etc.)
       System/firmware updates (drone and its components)
       Recordkeeping of all maintenance performed
       Cleaning procedures
   Maintenance should be done by qualified personnel.
   All maintenance should be documented – keep a log.
   If other team members complete the UA maintenance, the RPIC is still responsible and should verify it was done correctly before flight.
